walkthrough.title: Choose Your Villain
walkthrough.text: Let's get Disney Villainous set up! Each player picks a villain to play as. The base game includes Captain Hook, Jafar, Maleficent, Prince John, Queen of Hearts, and Ursula. Each villain plays completely differently, so take a moment to browse the options. For your first game, Captain Hook and Prince John are great beginner-friendly choices.

walkthrough.title: Collect Your Villain's Components
walkthrough.text: Once you have chosen your villain, collect everything that belongs to them. Each villain gets a realm board, a villain mover piece, a villain deck of thirty cards, a fate deck of fifteen cards, a villain guide, and a reference card. Keep your villain guide handy because it explains your unique win condition and special rules.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Realm Board
walkthrough.text: Set your realm board in front of you. This board shows four locations in your villain's realm, each with different action symbols on the top and bottom. Your villain mover starts on the leftmost location. If your rightmost location shows a lock icon, place a lock token on it. That location is unavailable until you unlock it.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Shuffle your villain deck and place it face down to the left of your realm board with room for a discard pile. Then shuffle your fate deck and place it face down to the right of your realm board with room for its own discard pile. These two decks serve very different purposes during the game.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Draw four cards from your villain deck. This is your starting hand. Look at your cards but keep them hidden from other players. Your villain deck contains allies, items, effects, and conditions that help you advance toward your objective.

walkthrough.title: Read Your Objective Aloud
walkthrough.text: This is an important step that many groups skip. Read your villain's win condition out loud so everyone at the table knows what you are trying to accomplish. This is critical because other players use the Fate action to slow you down, and they need to know your objective to do that effectively.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Shared Cauldron
walkthrough.text: Place the cauldron in the center of the table and fill it with all eighty power tokens. Power is the game's shared currency that every villain needs. You will gain power from your realm board actions and spend it to play cards from your villain deck.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Power
walkthrough.text: Each player receives starting power from the cauldron based on their seat position. The first player gets zero power, the second gets one, the third gets two, and the fourth gets two. For five or six players, the fifth gets three and the sixth gets three. This compensates later players for going second.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Fate Token
walkthrough.text: If you are playing with five or six players, place the Fate token near the center. When a player is targeted by a Fate action, they take the Fate token. While holding the Fate token, that player cannot be targeted by Fate again until someone else is Fated and takes it. This prevents pile-on in larger games.

walkthrough.title: Choose a First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick who goes first using any method you like. The first player has the advantage of acting first but starts with zero power. Play proceeds clockwise. On your first turn, you will move your villain mover and take the actions available at your chosen location.

bullets: 6 villains: Captain Hook, Jafar, Maleficent, Prince John, Queen of Hearts, Ursula
Beginner-friendly: Captain Hook, Prince John

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do three things in order. First, move your villain mover to a different location in your realm. Second, perform the available action symbols at that location in any order you choose. Third, draw cards from your villain deck until you have four in hand. Then play passes clockwise.

walkthrough.title: Moving Your Villain
walkthrough.text: You must move to a different location each turn. You cannot stay where you are. You can move to any location in your realm, not just adjacent ones. Your choice of location determines which actions you get this turn, so planning your movement path across multiple turns is one of the game's core skills.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Action Symbols
walkthrough.text: Each location has action symbols on the top and bottom halves. The top symbols are your normal actions. The bottom symbols are also available unless they are covered by Heroes. When another player Fates you, Heroes land on your locations and can block those bottom action symbols. This is the main way opponents slow you down.

walkthrough.title: Gain Power
walkthrough.text: The Gain Power action lets you take power tokens from the shared cauldron. The number shown on the symbol tells you how much you gain. Power is the currency you spend to play cards from your villain deck. Managing your power economy is essential because running out of power means dead turns.

walkthrough.title: Play a Card
walkthrough.text: The Play a Card action lets you pay a card's power cost and play it from your hand. Cards include allies who fight for you, items that enhance your board state, effects that trigger immediately, and conditions that create ongoing rules. Some cards must be played to specific locations while others are more flexible.

walkthrough.title: Activate an Ability
walkthrough.text: The Activate action lets you use a special ability printed on a card that is already in play. These abilities often have an additional cost. Not all cards have activated abilities, but the ones that do tend to be very powerful and worth building your strategy around.

walkthrough.title: Move an Item or Ally
walkthrough.text: This action lets you relocate one ally or item from one location to another within your realm. Positioning matters because many objectives require specific cards at specific locations. You also need allies in the right spots to vanquish Heroes that are blocking your action symbols.

walkthrough.title: Fate Another Player
walkthrough.text: Draw two cards from that player's fate deck and choose one to play against them. Fate cards include Heroes that block their action symbols, items that strengthen Heroes, and effects that disrupt their plans. The unchosen card goes to the bottom of their fate deck.

walkthrough.title: Vanquish a Hero
walkthrough.text: To remove a Hero from your realm, use the Vanquish action. You need allies at the same location as the Hero whose combined strength meets or exceeds the Hero's strength. After vanquishing, the Hero goes to the fate discard pile. Your allies stay on the board unless their card says otherwise.

walkthrough.title: Discard Cards
walkthrough.text: The Discard action lets you discard any number of cards from your hand. Since you draw back to four cards at the end of your turn, this is your way to cycle through your villain deck faster. Use it to dump cards that do not help your current strategy and dig for the pieces you need.

walkthrough.title: Move a Hero
walkthrough.text: This lets you relocate a Hero on your board to a different location. You might want to move a Hero off a critical location or move them to a location where you already have allies ready to vanquish. Not every villain board has this action, but it is very useful when available.

walkthrough.title: Heroes and Covered Symbols
walkthrough.text: When a Hero is placed on one of your locations by an opponent's Fate action, the Hero covers the bottom row of action symbols at that location. Those symbols become unavailable until the Hero is vanquished or moved. This is the core interaction between players. Placing Heroes on the right location can be devastating.

walkthrough.title: Villain-Specific Objectives
walkthrough.text: Each villain has a unique win condition. Captain Hook must defeat Peter Pan at the Jolly Roger. Jafar must control the Genie with the Magic Lamp at Sultan's Palace. Maleficent must have a Curse at every location. Prince John must start a turn with twenty or more power. These are all different puzzle paths.

walkthrough.title: Timing: When You Can Win
walkthrough.text: Some villains can win immediately when their condition is met. Others can only win at the start of their turn. Check your villain guide carefully because this timing distinction matters enormously. If your win condition says "at the start of your turn," your board state must survive a full round of opponents potentially Fating you.

walkthrough.title: Ending the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when any player fulfills their villain's objective at the correct timing. There are no victory points and no final scoring. The first villain to complete their objective wins. This makes Villainous a race game at its core, and Fate is the tool everyone uses to slow the leader.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your Villainous Realm
walkthrough.text: We will walk through a practice game with four players: Captain Hook, Jafar, Maleficent, and Prince John. Each villain has a completely different objective, so watch how each one approaches the game differently.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Captain Hook
walkthrough.text: Captain Hook goes first and moves to the Jolly Roger location. He uses the Gain Power action to take three power tokens. Then he plays a Swashbuckler ally card for its cost. His objective is to defeat Peter Pan at the Jolly Roger, so he is building up allies there early.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Jafar
walkthrough.text: Jafar moves to the Streets of Agrabah and gains power. He needs the Magic Lamp and the Genie, both of which are in his villain deck. His plan is to cycle cards quickly and find those two key pieces. He uses his Discard action to throw away two cards that do not help and draws back to four.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Maleficent
walkthrough.text: Maleficent moves to the Forbidden Mountains. She needs a Curse at each of her four locations to win. She plays a Curse card at the Forbidden Mountains. Three more to go. Maleficent's strategy is straightforward but she is vulnerable to Heroes removing her Curses.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Prince John
walkthrough.text: Prince John moves to the Jail and gains power. His objective is to start a turn with twenty or more power. He takes three power from the cauldron and now has five total. Prince John's game is all about accumulating and protecting his power stash from opponents.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Other Players
walkthrough.text: After a few rounds, Maleficent has placed three Curses and is one away from winning. Nobody has used the Fate action against her yet because everyone was focused on their own board. This is the most common beginner mistake. In Villainous, you must watch the other villains and slow down the leader.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Fate the Leader
walkthrough.text: Villainous is a race game, and the Fate action is your brakes on the leader. If one player is close to winning and nobody Fates them, they will cruise to victory unopposed. As a rule of thumb, always Fate the player closest to their win condition. It is not mean, it is the game's core balancing mechanic.

walkthrough.title: Captain Hook Uses Fate
walkthrough.text: Captain Hook moves to a location with the Fate symbol and targets Maleficent. He draws two cards from her fate deck and finds Aurora and Prince Phillip. He places Prince Phillip on the location where Maleficent has a Curse. Prince Phillip's ability removes the Curse. Now Maleficent is back to two Curses.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Forgetting Win Timing
walkthrough.text: Prince John accumulates twenty-two power during his turn and excitedly declares victory. But wait. His objective says he must start his turn with twenty or more power. He reached twenty during his turn, not at the start of it. He has to survive a full round of opponents potentially draining his power before he can actually win.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Start-of-Turn Timing
walkthrough.text: Several villains have start-of-turn win conditions, meaning their board state must survive from the end of their turn all the way around to the beginning of their next turn. This gives opponents a full round to disrupt you. Build a board state that can withstand Fate before revealing you are about to win.

walkthrough.title: Jafar Finds His Key Cards
walkthrough.text: Jafar finally draws the Magic Lamp. He plays it to the Cave of Wonders. Next he needs the Genie from his fate deck, but the Genie only appears when an opponent Fates Jafar. Jafar now wants to be Fated, which is a unique twist. He announces he is close to winning, hoping someone targets him.

walkthrough.title: The Race Tightens
walkthrough.text: All four villains are now making progress. The table dynamics shift constantly as players balance advancing their own objective with Fating whoever is closest to winning. Captain Hook finally gets Peter Pan to appear through Fate, Maleficent rebuilds her Curses, and Prince John hoards power while fending off theft effects.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Only Visiting Power Locations
walkthrough.text: Prince John keeps returning to the same two high-power locations every turn. He is earning power but missing out on critical actions like playing cards and moving allies. Your movement pattern should cover different locations to access a variety of actions, not just the ones that give the most power.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Movement Path
walkthrough.text: Think two or three turns ahead about which locations you need to visit. Each turn you must move to a different location, so plan a rotation that covers the actions you need. Power is important, but so are playing cards, moving allies, and vanquishing Heroes. A balanced rotation wins faster.

walkthrough.title: The Winning Move
walkthrough.text: Maleficent has all four Curses in place again. She vanquished the Hero blocking her last location last turn. Now it is the start of her turn, and all four Curses are still on the board. Her start-of-turn condition is met. Maleficent wins the game. The other players congratulate her on surviving the gauntlet.

walkthrough.title: Post-Game Discussion
walkthrough.text: After the game, the group discusses what happened. Captain Hook realizes he should have Fated Maleficent one more time. Prince John notes he focused too much on power and not enough on defense. Jafar acknowledges that his late-game surge was almost enough. Every game tells a different story.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have played through a complete game of Disney Villainous. You saw how each villain pursues a different objective, learned the importance of Fating the leader, and discovered why win timing matters so much. Every villain offers a fresh puzzle to solve.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Be Villainous
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on power management, movement planning, and when to Fate.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick rundown of all actions, terms, and villain objectives.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Learn Your Villain's Key Cards
walkthrough.text: Before the game starts, read through your villain guide carefully. Every villain has two or three critical cards that are essential to their win condition. Know which cards you are looking for so you can prioritize drawing and discarding to find them quickly.

walkthrough.title: Power Economy Matters
walkthrough.text: Power is the fuel that drives everything. Without power, you cannot play cards. Prioritize early locations that give you steady power income. Most villains need at least two or three power-generating turns before they can start executing their strategy. Do not overspend early.

walkthrough.title: Cycle Your Deck Aggressively
walkthrough.text: If your hand is full of cards that do not advance your objective, use the Discard action to throw them away and draw fresh cards. Many new players hold onto mediocre cards hoping they will be useful later. Cycling gets you to your key cards faster, and that is almost always worth the lost tempo.

walkthrough.title: Fate the Leader, Not the Weakest
walkthrough.text: Always target the player closest to winning with your Fate action. It can feel mean to pile on one person, but this is the game's core balancing mechanism. If you Fate the weakest player instead, the leader wins unopposed. Save your Fate for whoever is actually threatening to end the game.

walkthrough.title: Conceal Your Progress
walkthrough.text: Do not announce how close you are to winning unless your villain requires interaction from others. Build your board state quietly and let opponents focus their Fate actions on someone else. The less attention you draw, the fewer Heroes end up in your realm.

walkthrough.title: Plan Multi-Turn Sequences
walkthrough.text: Identify which actions you need in which order and chart a movement path through your realm. This prevents dead turns where you end up at a location with actions you do not need.

walkthrough.title: Vanquish Heroes Efficiently
walkthrough.text: Heroes block your bottom-row actions. Do not let them linger. Position allies in advance at locations where Heroes are likely to land. When a Hero appears, you want to vanquish it on your very next turn rather than spending multiple turns repositioning allies.

walkthrough.title: Know All Six Objectives
walkthrough.text: You should know every villain's win condition, not just your own. This lets you make smart Fate decisions. When you Fate someone, knowing their objective helps you choose which fate card to play and which location to place a Hero on for maximum disruption.

summary.title: Learn Your Villain's Key Cards
bullets: Read villain guide before starting
Identify 2-3 critical cards for your win condition

summary.title: Power Economy Matters
bullets: Prioritize power-generating locations early
Need 2-3 power turns before executing strategy

summary.title: Cycle Your Deck Aggressively
bullets: Discard cards that don't advance your objective
Cycling > holding mediocre cards
Faster access to key pieces

summary.title: Fate the Leader, Not the Weakest
bullets: Target player closest to winning
Core balancing mechanic — not mean, it's the game
Fating the weakest lets the leader win free

summary.title: Conceal Your Progress
bullets: Build board state quietly
Less attention = fewer Heroes in your realm
Let opponents focus Fate elsewhere

summary.title: Plan Multi-Turn Sequences
bullets: Chart movement path 2-3 turns ahead
Must move to different location each turn
Prevents dead turns at unneeded locations

summary.title: Vanquish Heroes Efficiently
bullets: Pre-position allies where Heroes will land
Vanquish next turn, not 3 turns later
Don't let blocked symbols linger

summary.title: Know All Six Objectives
bullets: Learn every villain's win condition
Smart Fate choices require knowing what hurts them most
Place Heroes on their critical locations

walkthrough.title: Tempo Concealment
walkthrough.text: Advanced players deliberately hide their progress. Instead of placing your third Curse as Maleficent immediately, hold it in hand until you can also play the fourth in quick succession. Revealing your proximity to winning invites concentrated Fate from the entire table.

walkthrough.title: Targeted Fate Placement
walkthrough.text: When you Fate an opponent, think carefully about where to place the Hero. Do not just drop them at any location. Place Heroes on locations with the most valuable bottom-row actions, especially Fate symbols and Play a Card symbols. Blocking someone's Fate symbol means they cannot slow other players either.

walkthrough.title: The Overkill Defense
walkthrough.text: If you have a start-of-turn win condition, build redundancy into your board state. Prince John should aim for twenty-five or more power, not exactly twenty. Maleficent should have spare Curse cards in hand. Jafar should have allies protecting the Lamp. The buffer absorbs one round of Fate disruption.

walkthrough.title: Fate Deck Knowledge
walkthrough.text: After you have played Villainous a few times, learn what is in each villain's fate deck. Knowing which Heroes and effects exist lets you Fate more effectively. For example, Jafar's fate deck contains cards that move the Lamp. Knowing this lets you time your Fate for maximum disruption.

walkthrough.title: Alliance Dynamics
walkthrough.text: In four to six player games, informal alliances form naturally. If two players are close to winning, the other players need to coordinate Fate actions. Communicate who is Fating whom so the leader does not slip through while two players target the same villain and leave the leader alone.

walkthrough.title: The Villain Counter Chart
walkthrough.text: Some matchups naturally favor one villain over another based on fate deck contents. Prince John struggles against theft effects. Captain Hook needs a specific Hero to appear. Understanding which villains counter each other helps you choose when to Fate and who your biggest threat is.

walkthrough.title: Action Economy Optimization
walkthrough.text: The strongest turns are ones where every action symbol at your chosen location is useful. Before moving, check all four locations and count how many useful actions each offers this turn. Move to the location with the highest useful action count. Wasting even one action symbol adds up over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Table Clock
walkthrough.text: Experienced players can estimate how many turns each villain needs to win. Track each opponent's progress and calculate their earliest possible win turn. This lets you prioritize Fate timing. If one player can win in two turns and another in four, the two-turn threat needs immediate Fate attention.

summary.title: Tempo Concealment
bullets: Hold key pieces in hand → play them in rapid succession
Revealing proximity invites concentrated Fate

summary.title: Targeted Fate Placement
bullets: Place Heroes on highest-value bottom-row actions
Block Fate symbols → opponent can't slow others either

summary.title: The Overkill Defense
bullets: Build redundancy: aim higher than minimum win condition
Buffer absorbs 1 round of Fate disruption
25 power > 20 power for Prince John

summary.title: Fate Deck Knowledge
bullets: Learn what's in each villain's fate deck
Time Fate for maximum disruption
Key cards can move items, remove allies, etc.

summary.title: Alliance Dynamics
bullets: Coordinate Fate targets in 4-6p games
Don't double-Fate one villain while leader escapes
Communicate who is targeting whom

summary.title: The Villain Counter Chart
bullets: Some fate decks naturally counter certain strategies
Prince John struggles vs. theft effects
Know which matchups favor you

summary.title: Action Economy Optimization
bullets: Count useful actions at each location before moving
Move to highest useful action count
Wasted symbols compound over the game

summary.title: Reading the Table Clock
bullets: Estimate each villain's turns-to-win
Prioritize Fate on shortest timeline
2-turn threat > 4-turn threat

entries.definition: A villain's personal board showing four locations, each with top-row and bottom-row action symbols. The villain mover travels between these locations each turn.
The sculpted piece representing your villain. Must be moved to a different location on your realm board at the start of each turn.
The shared currency stored in the cauldron. Gained through board actions and spent to play cards from your villain deck.
The shared supply holding all eighty power tokens. Players gain power from and return power to the cauldron.
A thirty-card deck unique to each villain containing allies, items, effects, and conditions that help achieve the villain's objective.
A fifteen-card deck unique to each villain containing Heroes, items, and effects used by opponents to disrupt that villain's plans.
A reference card explaining the villain's unique objective, win timing, and any special rules specific to that character.
Placed on a villain's rightmost location if it starts locked. The location and its actions are unavailable until the lock condition is met.
Used in five to six player games. The player targeted by Fate takes this token and is immune to further Fate actions until another player is Fated.
An action symbol that lets you take power tokens from the cauldron equal to the number shown on the symbol.
An action symbol that lets you pay a card's power cost and play it from your hand to a legal location or target.
An action symbol that lets you trigger a special ability on a card already in play, usually with an additional cost.
An action symbol that lets you draw two cards from another player's fate deck and play one against them. The other goes to the bottom of their fate deck.
An action symbol that lets you defeat a Hero at a location using allies whose combined strength meets or exceeds the Hero's strength.
An action symbol that lets you discard any number of cards from your hand. You draw back to four at end of turn, effectively cycling your deck.
An action symbol that lets you relocate one ally or item from one location to another within your realm.
An action symbol that lets you relocate a Hero from one location to another within your realm. Useful for positioning Heroes where allies can vanquish them.
A fate deck card placed on a villain's location by an opponent. Heroes cover bottom-row action symbols and must be vanquished to remove them.
A villain deck card representing a character who fights for you. Allies have a strength value used for vanquishing Heroes. Played to specific locations.
A card (from either deck) that adds a permanent effect or ability to a location. Items from the villain deck help you; items from the fate deck hinder you.
A card that triggers immediately when played and then is discarded. Effects can come from either the villain deck or the fate deck.
A villain deck card that creates an ongoing rule or triggered ability. Remains in play until its condition is met or it is removed.
Objective: Defeat Peter Pan at the Jolly Roger. A combat-focused villain who needs Peter Pan to appear through Fate before he can win.
Objective: Start your turn with the Magic Lamp at Sultan's Palace and the Genie under your control. A search-and-position puzzle villain.
Objective: Start your turn with a Curse at each of your four locations. A spread-and-protect strategy villain vulnerable to Hero removal.
Objective: Start your turn with at least twenty power. An accumulation villain who must defend his wealth from opponent disruption.
Objective: Have a Wicket at each location and successfully take a shot. A multi-step setup villain with a unique card-flip minigame.
Objective: Start your turn with the Trident and the Crown at Ursula's Lair. A retrieval and positioning villain who searches for two specific items.
When a Hero occupies a location, the bottom-row action symbols are covered and unavailable. Top-row symbols remain accessible.
A win condition that is only checked at the very beginning of a villain's turn. The board state must survive a full round of opponent actions.
